1:
The chapter opens with a king undone by a blasphemer's words, and the witnesses fasten on Hezekiah's threefold answer of grief, worship, and appeal. The reformed John Gill sets the scene plainly: what Hezekiah heard was the report of Rabshakeh's speech delivered in the preceding chapter, and the hearing of it moved him at once. The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke, casting his eye over the whole chapter, sees Hezekiah distressed and sending to Isaiah to pray, the prophet returning a comfortable answer, and the ruin of the Assyrian foretold — the shape of everything that follows.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry lingers longest, marking first what the king did not do. One would have expected him to call a council of war and debate whether to capitulate; before the siege he had taken counsel with his princes and mighty men, as 2 Chron. 32:3 records. But that would not serve now: his greatest relief is that he has a God to go to. Henry reads the rent clothes and sackcloth as the mark of a man wounded not for himself but for God — good men were wont so to grieve when they heard reproach cast on God's name, and royal robes are not too good to be rent, nor royal flesh too good for sackcloth. The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley says the same in a breath: great men must not think it any disparagement to sympathize with the injured honour of the great God. Henry adds that God was displeased with those not so affected, pointing to the feasting rebuked in Isa. 22:12-14, where joy and gladness filled the city on a day of trouble.

The second movement is worship, and here Henry meets the grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott. Ellicott glosses the act simply: Hezekiah went into the house of the LORD to humble himself before Jehovah and pray for help, comparing 2 Chron. 32:20. Henry likens him to the psalmist who, grieved at the prosperity of the wicked, went into the sanctuary and there understood their end, as in Ps. 73:17; in the house of God the king found rest and refuge, a council-chamber and all he needed, all in God. The third movement, the embassy to Isaiah, Henry and Gill take as the natural sequel: the king who prays himself also begs the prayers of the man who could pray better and had a better interest in heaven, for "he is a prophet, and he shall pray for thee," as was said of Abraham in Gen. 20:7. The witnesses do not divide; together they set faith's answer over against the blasphemer's threat.
2:
The verse names the honourable embassy Hezekiah sent to the prophet, and the witnesses weigh both its composition and what it signifies. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch begin, characteristically, with a point of Hebrew order: the phrase "Isaiah the prophet, the son of Amoz" is unusual, and is smoothed in the parallel of Isaiah 37:2 to "Isaiah the son of Amoz, the prophet." They record the message the envoys carried — that this was a day of trouble, of rebuke, and of blasphemy — reserving its fuller exposition for the next verse.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott dwells on the dignity of the delegation. Eliakim, who was over the household, and Shebna the scribe, and the elders of the priests were sent, and Ellicott, with Knobel, remarks that so distinguished an embassy speaks for the high estimation in which the prophet stood; he compares the sendings of 2 Kings 22:14 and Jer. 37:3. The "elders of the priests," he is careful to say, are the heads of the sacerdotal caste — the leading men, not merely aged men. That the messengers went covered with sackcloth was itself a message: they came clothed as the king was clothed, representing his humiliation before the prophet.

The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke draws out the spiritual force of the choice. Isaiah's fame and influence were at this time great in Israel, and it was well known that the word of the LORD was with him; here, Clarke observes, both the Church and the state unite in fervent application to and strong dependence upon God — "and behold how they succeed!" The witnesses converge on a single reading of the scene. The king does not first summon his generals but his prophet; the crown sends to the man of God, and sends in sackcloth, confessing that the deliverance it needs will come by prayer and not by policy. That a king should so honour a prophet, and a prophet be reckoned worth an embassy of the highest officers of the realm, tells the reader where the true strength of Judah was thought to lie — not in the counsel of Eliakim and Shebna, able men though they were, but in the intercession they were sent to secure.
3:
The envoys deliver their errand in a proverb of desperation, and the witnesses gather around its two vivid images — the day of distress and the child that cannot be born. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch parse the threefold name the messengers give the day: a day of trouble, of chastisement, and of rejection. The middle word denotes the divine chastisement, and the last, contemptuous treatment or rejection of the people on God's part, the same harsh term used in Deut. 32:19 and Jer. 14:21. Then comes the figure: children are come to the birth, and there is no strength to bring forth — a picture, they say, of extreme danger and the most desperate circumstances, when the travailing woman has not strength to deliver the child already come to the mouth of the womb.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott sharpens the same words. "Rebuke," he insists, is rather chastisement, the verb meaning to give judgment or punish, as in the next clause where God will "reprove," that is punish for, the words; and "blasphemy" he ties to the cognate language of Isa. 1:4 and to the "provocations" of Neh. 9:18. The proverb of the children come to the birth he compares with the like saying of the prophet in Hos. 13:13 — the utter collapse of all human resource.

The wesleyan-arminian witnesses press the image into feeling. Adam Clarke expands it with a scholar's relish, seeing the Jewish state as a woman in travail so long in her pangs that her strength is spent and her deliverance hopeless without a miracle, and he sets beside it the Homeric picture of Agamemnon's throes from the eleventh book of the Iliad — the same fierce, unutterable woe. John Wesley gives the proverb Judah's own voice: we are like a poor travailing woman in great extremity, having attempted to deliver ourselves from the Assyrian yoke and carried the work almost to the birth, but now we have no might to finish; we had begun a happy reformation and are hindered from bringing it to perfection. The witnesses do not divide. Together they read the message as a confession that the nation has come to the end of itself — brought to the very point of deliverance yet powerless to accomplish it, so that only God can bring to the birth what the people cannot.
4:
The heart of the embassy is a wish and a plea — "it may be the LORD thy God will hear" — and the witnesses weigh both the hope and the ground of it. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch read the opening "it may be" as the language of a man who reckons the Assyrian's words a real affront to heaven: perhaps Jehovah will hear the blasphemies of the living God on Rabshakeh's part, "hear" meaning here to take notice of and so to punish. The title "the living God," they observe, is set deliberately against the gods of the heathen, who are only lifeless idols, as in 1 Sam. 17:26; and "will reprove the words" they take as a progressive perfect — God will chastise him for the words He has heard. The prayer is then asked "for the remnant that is left," nearly all Judah having already fallen into Sennacherib's power.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott adds a fine gloss on the tone. Citing the old commentator Clericus, he says the "it may be" is "not the language of one who doubts, but of one who hopes." He construes "lift up thy prayer" heavenward, comparing the phrase to lifting up the voice, that is, to utter; and he gives the "remnant" its pathos — Sennacherib had captured most of the strong cities of Judah, so that, in the prophet's own image, "the daughter of Zion was left as a hut in a vineyard," as in Isa. 1:8.

The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley fixes the same word with a historian's precision: the remnant is Judah, which is but a remnant now that the ten tribes are gone, and Jerusalem, which is but a remnant now that the defenced cities of Judah are taken. The witnesses do not contend. They agree that the plea rests not on Judah's strength, which is spent, nor even on her righteousness, but on the honour of the living God whom the Assyrian has dared to reproach, and on the mercy that stoops to a mere surviving remnant. It is the prayer of men who have nothing left to plead but God's own name and God's own pity, and who are content, having spread the case, to leave the issue with Him who hears.
5:
With a single quiet sentence the narrative turns from the king's fear to the prophet's word — "so the servants of king Hezekiah came to Isaiah" — and the witnesses, few here, mark the hinge it forms. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott notes only, with disarming candour, that the verse merely resumes the narrative in a somewhat simple and artless fashion; it is the plain seam that joins the sending of the embassy to the answer about to be given, and Ellicott does not pretend it carries more than that. The verse is a breath drawn before the oracle.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch look past the seam to the reply it introduces, and lay out the comfort Isaiah returns. Hezekiah was not to be afraid of the blasphemous words of the Assyrian king; the LORD would put a spirit into him, so that he should hear a report and return to his own land, and there God would cause him to fall by the sword. They pause over the derogatory phrase for the Assyrian officials — literally the "young men" or servants of the king of Assyria — a contemptuous epithet flung back at those who had so proudly reproached the God of Israel.

Keil and Delitzsch are careful, too, about the "report" that would send him home. It cannot be, as Thenius supposed, the news of his army's destruction recorded at v.35, for Sennacherib did not hear of that through a messenger — he was with the army himself when the angel smote it. The report is rather the rumour of Tirhakah's advance noted in v.9; and though the king would make one last attempt on Jerusalem after hearing it, his courage, once shaken, would fail, and retreat would follow. The witnesses do not divide. They let this unassuming verse do its work as the threshold of the whole deliverance, where the servants who came in dread carry away a word that turns the siege: the God who was blasphemed will answer, and the answer is spoken before a single Assyrian has fallen. Keil and Delitzsch note, too, that Isaiah's reply already scorns the foe, calling Sennacherib's officers merely the servants or young men of the Assyrian king — a derogatory epithet flung back at the proud embassy that had swaggered before the walls.
6:
Isaiah's answer opens with the great imperative of the whole chapter — "be not afraid" — and the witnesses, though only two are at hand here, weigh both the comfort and the scorn folded into it. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch give the oracle its plain force: Hezekiah was not to be afraid of the words with which the servants of the king of Assyria had blasphemed the LORD. What strikes them is the deliberate belittling in that phrase. Sennacherib's spokesmen, who had swaggered before the walls of Jerusalem, are named merely the "servants" — the young men or underlings — of the Assyrian king, a contemptuous epithet that quietly cuts the proud embassy down to size even as it reports their blasphemy.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott presses the same word. "The servants," he says, is more special in sense than a common slave: it denotes a personal attendant, and Delitzsch would render it "squires," the term recurring for the young men who wait on a master, as in 2 Kings 4:12 and 2 Kings 5:20. And he marks a nicety easily lost in English: the verb here rendered "blasphemed" is not the same root as the "blasphemy" of v.3, but the harsher word used of the sinner who acts with a high hand against the LORD in Num. 15:30 — a reviling that reaches up against heaven itself.

The two witnesses do not divide, and their small philological notes carry a large comfort. The command "be not afraid" is grounded not in Judah's strength but in the smallness of the enemy before God: the men whose reviling had terrified a city are, in the divine reckoning, mere squires of a mortal king, and their high-handed blasphemy has been heard and marked by the very God they defied. Isaiah does not argue Hezekiah out of his fear with military calculation; he simply relocates the whole quarrel. The reproach was flung not really at Jerusalem but at the living God, and it is God who now answers it. The king may lay down his dread, for the case has passed out of his weak hands into the hands of One whom no Assyrian boast can move, and who has already resolved how the blasphemer shall be repaid.
7:
The oracle turns from comfort to concrete sentence — "I will send a blast upon him" — and the witnesses divide, mildly, over what that mysterious "blast" or "spirit" is. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott renders it "I am about to put a spirit within him," and reads the word not personally but in the weaker sense of an impulse or inclination, a mood of despondency and fear that Jehovah would breathe into the king, so that on hearing ill news he would hastily retire; he compares the disabling "spirit" of Isa. 19:14. Behind the psychology stands a conviction Ellicott shares with the prophets: that all acts and events, even the terrors that move a conqueror's heart, are Jehovah's work.

The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke reads the two clauses forward into the history and finds each fulfilled. The "rumour" the king should hear was the report that Tirhakah had come out against him, noticed in v.9; the "blast" was that which slew a hundred and eighty-five thousand of his men in one night, as v.35 will tell; and the falling "by the sword in his own land" pointed to his murder by his own sons at Nineveh, recorded in v.37. The one short oracle, Clarke shows, gathers up the rumour, the slaughter, and the assassination that the rest of the chapter will unfold.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch hold the two readings together without strain. The spirit put into Sennacherib works precisely through the report: in consequence of the despondency God breathes into him, he retires upon hearing the news, and the retreat, once begun in fear, is only hastened by the blow that falls on his army. So the witnesses converge on a single point even where their emphases differ. Whether one dwells, with Ellicott, on the inward "spirit" of dread or, with Clarke, on the outward blast, rumour, and sword, the verse teaches that God will turn the great king by unseen means — a whisper of fear, a distant rumour, a stroke in the night — and bring him home to die. The deliverance of Jerusalem is decreed before it is enacted, and its instruments are already named.
8:
The scene shifts to the Assyrian camp, and the witnesses trace the movements that the bare verse compresses. The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry sets the stage: Rabshakeh, having delivered his message and received no answer, left the army before Jerusalem under the other generals and went to attend his master for further orders, finding him no longer at Lachish but besieging Libnah, a city that had revolted from Judah, as noted in 2 Kings 8:22. Whether Lachish had been taken or abandoned as impracticable Henry leaves open; what matters is that the king, now alarmed by the rumour of the Cushite advance, was eager to gain Jerusalem quickly, and, having once found Hezekiah an easy man in 2 Kings 18:14, hoped to frighten him into surrender again — but in vain.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott reads the verse as taking up the narrative from 2 Kings 18:37, and infers that Tartan and Rabsaris and the great host departed with Rabshakeh, foiled of their purpose. Libnah he locates by his note on 2 Kings 8:22; and he takes it as established that the great king had by now taken Lachish, comparing 2 Chron. 32:9, so that Sennacherib had already begun to move — Schrader even placing Libnah at Tell-es-Safieh, as if the retreat were under way.

The wesleyan-arminian witnesses fill in the geography and the errand. Adam Clarke observes that Libnah and Lachish were not far apart, both in the mountains of Judah southward of Jerusalem; John Wesley notes simply that Rabshakeh returned to the king to give an account of the treaty, leaving the army behind under the other commanders. The witnesses do not divide. Together they show a campaign still in motion — the spokesman recalled, the host redeploying, one fortress perhaps taken and another under siege — and behind all the marching, the quiet approach of the rumour that will unnerve the king. The verse that seems mere troop-movement is in truth the first turning of the tide Isaiah has just foretold, the great machine of Assyria beginning, without knowing it, to wheel toward home.
9:
The rumour Isaiah foretold now arrives in the person of Tirhakah, and the witnesses turn historian, fixing the man and the moment. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch identify Tirhakah as king of Cush, the Tarakos of Manetho, successor of Shebek, third king of the twenty-fifth or Ethiopian dynasty, whose name is spelt Tharqo upon the monuments and who is depicted on the great temple at Medinet-Abu cutting down the enemies of conquered lands before the god Ammon — a great conqueror, in Strabo's phrase, now advancing to make war on Sennacherib. On hearing of him the Assyrian sent messengers a second time to Hezekiah.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott confirms and enlarges the identification. Tirhakah is the Taharka of the Egyptian inscriptions, the Tarqu of the Assyrian, last king of the twenty-fifth Ethiopian dynasty and son of Shabataka the son of Shabaka, whom 2 Kings 17:4 had named; Sennacherib himself does not name him but calls him "the king of Meluhha," that is Meroë. Ellicott follows the later Assyrian records — Esarhaddon and Assurbanipal both waged further wars against this same Tirhakah — and notes the suggestion of Ewald and Knobel that the embassy of Isaiah 18 reflects an approach from Tirhakah seeking Judah's help against the common foe, though he cautions that Egyptian chronology is too uncertain to bear much weight.

The two witnesses do not divide, and their patient scholarship serves the narrative more than it may first appear. The advance of the Ethiopian king is the very "report" that v.7 had promised would reach Sennacherib and turn him back; the second, more threatening letter he now sends to Hezekiah is the reaction of a man who feels the ground shifting beneath him and hastens, before the new enemy is upon him, to secure the surrender he could not win by his first embassy. So the monuments of Medinet-Abu and the annals of Assurbanipal, in the witnesses' hands, converge on the single point of the story: the God who said He would put a spirit of retreat into the great king has begun to do it by the rising of a rival empire in the south.
10:
Sennacherib's second message opens with a taunt aimed straight at Hezekiah's faith — "let not thy God in whom thou trustest deceive thee" — and the witnesses read it as blasphemy grown bolder. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch paraphrase the thrust: do not let your confidence in your God betray you into believing that Jerusalem shall not be given into the Assyrian's hand. To shatter that confidence the king recites the deeds of his predecessors, and Keil marks the emphatic verb he uses — to put under the ban, to smite with utter destruction — culminating in the scornful question, "and shalt thou be delivered?" a question framed to imply its own crushing negative.

The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke observes that this letter is nearly the same as the speech Rabshakeh had already made, comparing 2 Kings 18:29, where the same voice urged the people not to let Hezekiah deceive them. What had been said to the crowd at the wall is now written to the king in his palace; the argument has not changed, only its target. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott sharpens the insult of the phrase "deceive thee": Sennacherib means whatever channel Hezekiah's God might use — through prophets, or dreams, or any other recognised medium of communication — as if every oracle of comfort were a lie waiting to betray him.

The witnesses do not divide, and together they expose the peculiar wickedness of the letter. Rabshakeh had warned the people against trusting their king; Sennacherib now warns the king against trusting his God, and in doing so lifts the old contest onto its true plane. The Assyrian's whole strategy is to sever Judah from her one refuge, to make faith itself seem the fatal deception; and Clarke's cross-reference quietly reminds the reader that this is merely the wall-speech written large. Yet the very repetition betrays the enemy's weakness — he has no new argument, only a louder blasphemy — and by aiming his mockery at the living God rather than at the man, he draws down upon himself the answer the next verses will record. The taunt "let not thy God deceive thee" will prove, in the event, the truest word Sennacherib never meant to speak: it was he, not Hezekiah, who was deceived.
11:
The letter presses its case with a catalogue of ruined nations — "thou hast heard what the kings of Assyria have done to all lands" — and the witnesses attend to both the boast and the grim word that carries it. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch fix on the verb: the kings of Assyria destroyed all lands by "devoting them to the ban," an unsparing extermination, and on that basis Sennacherib flings his sneering "and shalt thou be delivered?" The rhetorical question is meant to leave no room for hope; if no land has escaped the Assyrian, why should Judah be the exception? Keil then follows the roll of conquered places into the next verse — Gozan, Haran, Rezeph, and the children of Eden — treating the list as a historical, not merely geographical, parade of victims.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott renders the crucial phrase with equal precision: "all the countries, by putting them under the ban," that is, by solemnly devoting all that lived in them to extermination. The horror of the boast, in his reading, is not merely conquest but consecrated slaughter — whole peoples vowed to destruction as a religious act. Ellicott's brevity here is itself pointed: the verse needs little comment beyond the exposure of what "destroying them utterly" actually means in the mouth of an Assyrian king.

The witnesses do not divide, and their agreement lets the verse's irony stand clear. Sennacherib appeals to a record of total, God-defying destruction as though it settled the matter — as though the God of Israel were merely one more tutelary deity to be swept away with Gozan and Haran. Keil's carrying of the argument into the list of fallen cities underscores the king's logic: an unbroken chain of victories, each sealed by the ban. But the reader, and the witnesses, know what Sennacherib does not — that the very ruthlessness he parades is the measure of the pride that heaven is about to break. The boast of having put all lands under the ban will be answered by the One who can put an army of a hundred and eighty-five thousand under a sterner ban in a single night, and the question "shalt thou be delivered?" will receive its reply not from Hezekiah but from God.
12:
Sennacherib turns from lands to their gods — "have the gods of the nations delivered them?" — reeling off the names of fallen peoples, and the witnesses supply the history the taunt assumes. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott does the antiquarian work with relish: "my fathers" means his predecessors generally, though Sargon founded the dynasty; Gozan he ties to 2 Kings 17:6; Haran is the west-Aramean town with its famous sanctuary of the moon-god Sin, near which Terah died in Gen. 11:31; Rezeph is the Assyrian Rasappa; the children of Eden he identifies with Bit-Adini, the "house of Eden" whose prince Ahuni fell to Shalmaneser; and Thelasar is the Assyrian Tul-Assuri, the "Mound of Assur."

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch handle the same list but with an eye to the argument's grammar, taking the pronoun "them" to reach back to the "all lands" of the previous verse, now itemized in the roll of conquered peoples. They too locate Haran as the Carrae of the Greeks and Romans in northern Mesopotamia, and treat the catalogue as historical rather than geographical — a parade of nations whose tutelary gods proved powerless before the advancing Assyrian.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry lifts the verse from geography to its blasphemous point. Sennacherib boasts, Henry says, of the gods he had conquered: each vanquished nation and its gods, which were so far from being able to deliver their worshippers that they fell together with them — "and shall thy God deliver thee?" That, Henry sees, is the whole venom of the list. The proud king ranges the God of Israel among the beaten idols of Gozan and Haran, reckoning Jehovah one more helpless patron to be swept away with the rest. The witnesses do not divide. Ellicott and Keil establish that the fallen cities were real and the conquests genuine, while Henry names the fatal confusion beneath the boast: to number the living God with the gods that are no gods is the very error that will bring Sennacherib to ruin, for the God he lists among the vanquished is the One who has heard his words and will presently answer them.
13:
The letter ends with a last roll of vanished kings — "where is the king of Hamath, and the king of Arpad?" — and the verse then carries Hezekiah up to the temple with the letter in his hand; the witnesses attend to both the taunt and the turn. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott catches a subtlety in the word "king." Comparing 2 Kings 18:34 and the flow of thought here, he judges that "king" is used as a synonym for the local god of each city — the tutelary deity spoken of as its sovereign, much as the psalmist joins the words in "my King, and my God" in Ps. 5:2, and as Amos couples king and god in Amos 5:26. The scornful "where is the king of Hamath?" thus asks not only after fallen princes but after impotent gods.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch carry the verse across its great hinge. Hezekiah received the letter from the hand of the messengers and read it, then went up into the house of the LORD and spread it open before the LORD. They note that the Hebrew word for the letter is plural, like the Latin litterae, which accounts for the shifting suffixes — a singular when the letter is spread out, since it was one document, though the envoys had first delivered its substance by word of mouth and handed over the writing as confirmation. The spreading of it before God, they add, was the embodiment of a child-like and trustful wish to lay the whole matter open before Him.

The witnesses do not divide, and the two halves of the verse answer each other. Sennacherib's letter had ranged the God of Israel among the beaten "kings" of Hamath and Arpad — gods that could not save their cities; Hezekiah's response is to carry that very letter into the house of the true God and lay it open before Him. Where the Assyrian asked "where is the king of Hamath?" the king of Judah shows by his act where his own King is — enthroned in the temple, neither deaf nor absent, able to read what is spread before Him. The blasphemer's catalogue of dead gods becomes, in Hezekiah's hands, the occasion for appealing to the living One, and the letter meant to break his faith becomes the very thing he brings to God in prayer.
14:
The verse lingers on a single eloquent gesture — Hezekiah spreading the letter open before the LORD — and the witnesses probe what the act meant and what it did not mean. The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke reads it against the temple's significance: the house of the LORD was reckoned God's dwelling-place, and whatever was there was peculiarly under His eye; so Hezekiah spread the letter before the LORD as one who wished Him to read the blasphemies spoken against Himself. The king does not answer Sennacherib in Sennacherib's own railing language; he lays the insult where it belongs, in the presence of the God it dishonoured, and leaves it there to be seen.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott takes up the same act but guards it against a crude misreading. The Hebrew word for the letter is plural, like the Latin litterae, and the shifting pronouns reflect that; the substance of the writing had first been delivered orally by the envoys and then the document produced to authenticate it. Some commentators, Ellicott notes, have taken offence at the spreading-out, as if it betokened a heathenish notion of God — Reuss mocking that Hezekiah did it "very naïvely, so that God might read it too." But this is unjust: one who could think of his God as having made heaven and earth, and as the only God, as the very next verse shows, would never imagine Him ignorant of a letter's contents until it was laid before Him. The act was a solemn and perfectly natural indication to his ministers and people that he had put the whole matter into Jehovah's hands.

The two witnesses do not divide; between them they hold the gesture in its true proportion. Clarke draws out its warmth — the wronged king bringing the reproach of God straight into God's house — while Ellicott defends its theology, insisting the spreading of the letter was no attempt to inform an ignorant deity but a public committal of the cause to One who already knew every word. The letter that Sennacherib meant to crush Hezekiah's confidence becomes the visible pledge of that confidence, unrolled on the temple floor as if to say that the quarrel is no longer the king's but God's, and the answer will be God's too, as the prayer of v.15 is about to make plain.
15:
Hezekiah's prayer begins with adoration, and the witnesses dwell on the great names he heaps upon God before he asks anything at all. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch hear in the address a deliberate answer to Assyrian delusion: the king describes Jehovah, the God of Israel, as the only God of all the kingdoms of the earth, because He is the Creator of heaven and earth. The title "who dwellest between the cherubim," they say, points to the covenant-relation into which the almighty Creator had entered with Israel — as the enthroned covenant God He was bound to help His people, and as the only God of the world He had the power to do it; the parallel in Isaiah even adds "of hosts," setting Jehovah at the outset as God of the universe.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott renders the phrase more exactly as "which sittest above the cherubim," the cherub-throned One, comparing Exod. 25:22 and 1 Sam. 4:4 He lays the emphasis where the Hebrew does — "Thou art the God, thou alone, unto all the kingdoms" — and reads "thou hast made heaven and earth" as the ground of it: because Thou it was that madest them, therefore Thou art the only God for all the kingdoms, as the prophet argues in Isa. 40:18.

The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley gathers the same titles into a confession over against Sennacherib's error. God is called the God of Israel because Israel was His peculiar people, yet He is the God of the whole earth, not, as Sennacherib fancied, the God of Israel only, confined to one land. "Let them say what they will," Wesley urges in the king's spirit, "thou art sovereign Lord, the God of gods, even thou alone: universal Lord of all the kingdoms of the earth, and rightful Lord; for thou hast made heaven and earth" — being Creator of all, by an incontestable title the owner and ruler of all. The witnesses do not divide. Together they show that Hezekiah's prayer opens by overturning the whole premise of the Assyrian letter: the God whom Sennacherib had listed among beaten local deities is the one Creator and covenant King, above the cherubim and above the kingdoms, and it is to Him, not to a tribal patron, that the case is now brought.
16:
From adoration the prayer presses to entreaty — "bow down thine ear, and hear; open thine eyes, and see" — and the witnesses read the piled-up imperatives as the marks of a real urgency. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch hear in the accumulation of verbs the earnestness and importunity of the prayer. They note the careful grammar: the plural "thine eyes" stands beside the singular "thine ear," because a man opens both eyes to see a thing but turns one ear to a speaker, and they observe that "hear the words of Sennacherib" fixes the whole petition on the blasphemy the letter contained, comparing the like language of Ps. 17:6.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott draws out what Hezekiah most wants God to hear and see. "Bow down thine ear" asks God to hear not so much the king's prayer as the words of Sennacherib; "open thine eyes" refers to the very letter, spread open in the temple. Ellicott is careful with the figure: to treat such language as though it meant God's eyes had literally been closed does violence to common sense; in the king's mouth "bow thine ear" and "open thine eyes" simply mean "intervene actively between me and my enemy." He corrects the last clause, too — better "which he hath sent" than "which hath sent him" — the words being regarded as a single message, sent by Sennacherib to reproach the living God, set in contrast with the lifeless idols of Hamath and Arpad.

The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley adds one small, telling observation. When Hezekiah prays that God would hear "the words of Sennacherib, which he hath sent to reproach the living God," the pronoun "him" in the fuller phrase refers to Rabshakeh — and the king, Wesley notes, would not do him the honour to name him. The witnesses do not divide. Together they show a prayer that asks God chiefly to attend not to the petitioner's distress but to the enemy's blasphemy: Hezekiah is content to let his own case rest if only God will look upon the reproach flung at His name. The urgency of the heaped imperatives is not the panic of a man pleading for his life but the zeal of a servant jealous for his Master's honour, confident that the living God, unlike the idols, both hears and sees.
17:
Hezekiah concedes the Assyrian's boast even as he empties it of force — "of a truth, LORD, the kings of Assyria have destroyed the nations" — and the witnesses weigh the candour of the admission. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch note that the king grants the fact plainly: the Assyrians have really devastated all lands, so that their vaunt of mighty power is not without ground. But he finds the explanation in the impotence and nothingness of the gods of the heathen. Keil observes that the parallel in Isaiah reads more sharply, "they have devastated all lands and their own land" — the harder and more original phrase, softened here — and that the point of the concession is to set up the contrast with the true God who cannot be so destroyed.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott reads the opening word in the same spirit: "of a truth" means "it is even as Sennacherib boasteth." Hezekiah does not dispute the conquests. Ellicott would render "destroyed" rather as "laid waste," and suggests the phrase may again be the language of the ban, the devoting of conquered peoples to destruction that ran through the earlier verses; "their lands" he takes distributively, of each conquered country in turn. The admission is unflinching — the Assyrian has indeed laid the nations waste.

The two witnesses do not divide, and the force of their agreement lies in what the concession prepares. Hezekiah's honesty is itself an argument: he will not pretend the Assyrian weak, for the whole weight of his prayer rests on distinguishing the living God from the gods that fell. Yes, the kings of Assyria have swept the nations away — but only because those nations trusted in idols that were no gods at all, mere works of men's hands, as the next verse will say. By granting the boast in full, the king throws into relief the one thing the boast cannot touch: that the God of Israel is not of that company, and that the very completeness of the Assyrian's past victories, far from threatening Judah, only sets the stage for the God who made heaven and earth to show the difference between Himself and the wood and stone that could not save Gozan or Haran.
18:
The prayer explains the Assyrian's success by the nothingness of the gods that fell — "they were no gods, but the work of men's hands, wood and stone" — and here the witnesses find both a theology and a critical puzzle. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch read the clause as the ground of Hezekiah's whole plea: the heathen deities were cast into the fire and destroyed precisely because they were not gods but human handiwork, and the king moves at once from this to his petition, "now therefore save thou us, that all the kingdoms of the earth may know that thou art the LORD God, even thou only" — the deliverance sought not for safety's sake alone but that God's sole deity might be owned everywhere.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott presses the same confession and then raises the question it provokes. The idea that the idols "were no gods," he says, is common in the latter half of Isaiah, and the Assyrian's own scornful "where are the gods?" implied their annihilation; he compares the burning of captured gods in 1 Chron. 14:12 and the polemic of Isa. 2:18. But Ellicott candidly airs the critic's doubt — whether the compiler has made Hezekiah express a stricter monotheism than the religious thought of his day had reached — and answers it by pointing to the herdsman-prophet Amos, who already proclaimed the one Creator who brought the Philistines from Caphtor as surely as Israel from Egypt, as in Amos 5:8; though he grants the prayer may be a free composition set in the king's mouth by the narrator.

The witnesses do not truly divide, and even Ellicott's critical scruple rests on the point they share, that the confession is genuinely of a piece with the prophetic faith of the age. Keil takes the words at face value as Hezekiah's own; Ellicott entertains the question of their exact authorship but affirms their theology by the witness of Amos. What neither doubts is the argument itself: the gods of the nations perished because they were wood and stone, and the God of Israel will save because He alone is God. The prayer's aim rises above Judah's rescue to the knowledge of God among all the kingdoms — that the deliverance of one small city might publish to the world the truth the idols could never defend.
20:
The scene turns from the temple to the prophet's house, and the witnesses mark that God's answer follows hard upon the prayer. The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry calls it a gracious and copious answer: the earlier message by the same hand in v.6 might have seemed reply enough, but that Hezekiah might have strong consolation he is now encouraged by two immutable things, in which it was impossible for God to lie, as in Heb. 6:18. In sum God assures him that his prayer against Sennacherib is heard; and Henry draws the sober lesson that the case of those who have the prayers of God's people against them is miserable, for if the oppressed cry to God against the oppressor He will hear, as He warned in Exod. 22:23, and He hears with the saving strength of His right hand, as in Ps. 20:6.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott attends to the mechanics of the moment. Isaiah, as Hezekiah's trusted adviser, may well have counselled the king to go up to the house of the LORD, or at least would have known his intention; and now the prophet "sent," that is caused to be handed over, a divine revelation concerning the hearing of the prayer. Ellicott renders the connecting phrase "in regard to" or "touching that which thou hast prayed," and notes a textual point — the verb "I have heard" has dropped out of the parallel in Isa. 37:21, though it stands here to make the sense plain.

The two witnesses do not divide. Henry supplies the theology of the answer — that a prayer laid before God against a blasphemer is a prayer already half-answered, since God binds Himself to hear the cry of the wronged — while Ellicott supplies its setting, the prophet relaying heaven's word to the waiting king. Between them the verse becomes the pivot of the chapter: Hezekiah had spread his letter before God and asked Him to hear and see; now Isaiah brings back the assurance that God has done both. What follows in the great oracle against Sennacherib is not a new hope but the unfolding of this one sentence — "that which thou hast prayed to me I have heard" — the certain ground on which every later promise of deliverance securely stands.
21:
The oracle opens not with threat but with laughter — "the virgin the daughter of Zion hath despised thee" — and the witnesses savour the reversal. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch explain the figure: daughter Zion, daughter Jerusalem, is not merely the inhabitants but the city itself, pictorially personified as a maiden. Zion is called a "virgin" as an inviolable city the Assyrians cannot conquer, and her shaking of the head is a gesture of derision and pleasure at another's misfortune, as in Ps. 22:8; the phrase "behind thee" they place first for emphasis, picturing the scorn that follows the enemy as he turns to go.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott confirms the personification — Zion as mother of her people, comparing 2 Sam. 20:19 — and stresses that "virgin" denotes the inviolable security of the citadel of Jehovah; the people of Jerusalem nod in scorn at the retiring envoys of Sennacherib. He cites Cheyne's verdict that the prophecy which follows is of striking interest, stamped in form and matter with the mark of Isaiah.

The wesleyan-arminian witnesses add colour and moral weight. Adam Clarke paraphrases the scorn boldly: so contemptible is the Assyrian's power that even the young women of Jerusalem, under the guidance of Jehovah, shall be enough to discomfit his forces and send him home in shame — a text so apt, Clarke recalls, that a young woman of Gloucester once took it as the motto of a pamphlet answering Bishop Warburton. John Wesley gives the title "virgin" its spiritual sense: Zion is so called because she was pure, in good measure, from the gross idolatry of the nations, and because God would defend her from Sennacherib's intended violation with no less care than parents guard their virgin daughters. The witnesses do not divide. Together they turn the tables on the great king: the city he thought to ravish laughs at his retreat, and the mightiest army on earth becomes the sport of an undefended maiden — because behind her stands the God whose honour was pledged when the letter was spread before Him. Keil and Delitzsch add that the shaking of the head is the gesture of open derision and malicious joy the psalms know well, as in Ps. 109:25, here directed at the envoys as they slink away.
22:
The taunt now names the Assyrian's real crime — "whom hast thou reproached and blasphemed?" — and the witnesses fix the answer that stings. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch read the question as falling upon Sennacherib for having blasphemed the Lord God by his foolish boasting of irresistible power. Lifting up the voice, they explain, refers to the threatening, assumptive tone in which he and Rabshakeh had spoken; lifting up the eyes on high means simply looking up to the sky, as in Isa. 40:26, not, as some would have it, directing proud looks against God. The arrogance lies not in the gesture but in its object — "against the Holy One of Israel," the God who manifests His holiness in and upon His people.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott agrees that "on high" means toward heaven, comparing again Isa. 40:26 and the self-exalting boast of Isa. 14:13-14. He dwells on the title that closes the verse: "the Holy One of Israel" is a favourite expression of Isaiah's, occurring twenty-seven times in his book and only five times elsewhere in the Old Testament, among them Ps. 71:22 — a mark, in Ellicott's eyes, of the oracle's genuine Isaianic stamp.

The two witnesses do not divide, and their close reading sharpens the verse's point. Sennacherib had raised his voice and lifted his eyes as though there were nothing above him; but the God he affected not to see is the Holy One of Israel, whose very name binds His holiness to the people the Assyrian meant to devour. The question "whom hast thou reproached?" is not rhetorical bluster but a summons to judgment: the king had imagined himself measuring swords with a petty local deity, and is told that he has lifted his eyes against the Creator who numbers the stars. To blaspheme the Holy One of Israel is to challenge the one power that no empire has ever overcome, and the rest of the oracle will make plain how that challenge is answered. Ellicott counts the title 'Holy One of Israel' but five times outside Isaiah's own book, among them Jer. 50:29, so that its very rarity elsewhere stamps this prophecy as the prophet's own.
23:
The oracle now quotes the great king's own swagger back to him — "by thy messengers thou hast reproached the Lord" — and the witnesses unpack the boast of mountains and forests. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch summarize it: Sennacherib had said that with the multitude of his chariots he had climbed to the height of the mountains and the sides of Lebanon, cut down its tall cedars and choice firs, and entered its farthest lodging and the forest of its Carmel — a vaunt that no barrier of nature could stop him, which they carry forward into the boast of drying up rivers as the intended conquest of Egypt.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott reads the imagery as figure, not mere geography. Lebanon, as the northern bulwark of the land, stands for the whole country, as in Zech. 11:1; and the cedars and firs he cut down symbolise, in Isaiah's manner, the kings, princes, and nobles, all that is highest and stateliest, as in Isa. 2:13. The "lodging of his borders" he takes for Mount Zion or Jerusalem, and "Carmel" for a pleasure-garden or park — the royal palace and its grounds, like the house of the forest of Lebanon in 1 Kings 7:2. The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke reads the tall cedars and choice firs in the same way, as the princes and nobles of the country, and renders "the forest of his Carmel" better as "the forest and his fruitful field."

The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley keeps closer to the literal march: Sennacherib boasts that he has brought his very chariots up to mountains thought inaccessible, felled the trees that hindered his army, and levelled a road through the cedars of Lebanon, resolving to overrun the whole land from border to border. The witnesses do not finally divide — some hearing figures of conquered nobility, others the plain brag of an unstoppable march — but all agree on the spirit of the speech. It is the language of a man who has made himself a god, who speaks of scaling heaven's own heights and stripping the earth at will; and the oracle repeats it only to expose it, setting the king's "I am come up, I have cut down, I have entered" against the "I have done it" of the Lord that the next verses will thunder in reply.
24:
The boast reaches its height in the claim to command even the waters — "I have digged and drunk strange waters" — and the witnesses read it as the vaunt of a conqueror who reckons nature itself his servant. The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke takes the first clause of conquest and supply: to have digged and drunk strange waters is to have subdued strange countries, digging wells for his army where he passed, or gaining the wealth of foreign lands; and the drying up of rivers "with the sole of my feet" he refers to an infantry so vast that its mere marching could drink a river dry. John Wesley reads similarly — strange waters never discovered by others, and rivers dried up by the tramp of a numberless host, each soldier bearing away his part, or new channels cut to drain them.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott presses toward the specific target the imagery hides. Scarcity of water, Sennacherib boasts, has been no bar to his advance: in foreign and hostile lands, where the fountains and cisterns were stopped and covered as in 2 Chron. 32:3, he had digged new wells; and "I will dry up with the sole of my feet all the Nile-arms of Mazor" he takes to mean Lower Egypt, comparing Isa. 19:5. Neither mountain nor river, the king claims, can stop his progress, whether across the waterless desert of the Tih between Egypt and Palestine or through the very streams of the Nile. Ellicott notes that the poetical perfects may stand for a boasted future — "I will ascend, I will dig, I will dry up."

The witnesses do not divide, and their notes let the blasphemy stand naked. Whether the strange waters are the wells of the desert or the branches of the Nile, the point of the boast is the same: Sennacherib claims a power over creation that belongs to its Maker alone. He will climb the inaccessible mountains, fell the cedars, drink the rivers dry — as though the God who set the bounds of the sea were not above him. It is precisely this arrogation of divine mastery that the oracle has been quoting in order to shatter, for the next verse turns from the king's "I have digged, I have dried up" to the Lord's rebuke: that all he did, he did only because God long before had appointed it.
25:
God breaks in upon the boasting with a question that overturns it — "hast thou not heard long ago how I have done it?" — and the witnesses gather at the turn from the king's "I" to the Lord's. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch set the divine purpose against the human vaunt: what Sennacherib claimed as his own achievement was in truth formed by God from the days of old and only now brought to pass, the Assyrian acting, without knowing or owning it, simply as the instrument of the Lord who had given him power to destroy — power God would soon restrain when the ranting reached too high. The fortified cities laid in ruinous heaps were levelled by a hand above the king's.

The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke makes the point explicit: here Jehovah speaks, and shows this boasting king that all he had done was done by divine appointment; of his own counsel and might he could have accomplished nothing. It was because God had appointed the nations to this judgment that Sennacherib overcame them; he only got the victory over men whom God had first confounded and enervated, as the next verse will say. John Wesley presses it home in the second person: hast thou not long since learned, what even thy philosophers could teach, that there is a supreme God by whose decree all these wars are ordered, whose mere instrument thou art? "This work is mine, not thine" — I have so disposed things by my providence that thou shouldest be a victorious prince, first against Israel and now against Judah.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott renders the sense crisply: "in the far past I made it; in the days of yore did I fashion it; now have I brought it to pass," the "it" being the long-foreordained purpose defined by the clause that follows — that Sennacherib should be the one to lay the fenced cities waste, as the prophet argues in Isa. 22:11 and the parable of the axe and the rod in Isa. 10:5-15. The witnesses do not divide. With one voice they strip the conqueror of his glory: every mountain he climbed and city he crushed was a task assigned him from of old, and the tool that boasts against the hand that wields it has forgotten who gave it an edge. The great king is not the author of history but its hired sword, and the God he blasphemed is the One who has been writing the story all along.
26:
The oracle explains why the nations fell so easily — "their inhabitants were of small power, they were dismayed and confounded" — and the witnesses read it as the unmasking of Assyria's supposed might. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch gloss the key phrase "of short hand," that is, without power to offer successful resistance, comparing Isa. 50:2. The conquered peoples were as perishable as the herbage of the field, the green herb, the grass on the housetops that withers before it grows, as in Ps. 129:6 and Isa. 40:6; and "blighted corn before the stalk" means corn withered up before it can shoot into stem. The nations Sennacherib overran were already grass God had let wither.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott renders "of small power" literally as "short-handed," and turns the phrase with a scholar's wit: Keil compares the well-known title of Artaxerxes Longimanus, the "long-handed," as though that epithet meant far-reaching in power — the conquered peoples being its opposite, men whose reach had failed. He reads the string of images as pictures of quick withering — field-growth and green herbage, grass of the roofs, blasting before the stalk — and notes that the parallel in Isa. 37:27 reads "field" for "blasting," which Thenius would adjust to yield "a field before the east wind."

The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley draws the theological nerve of the "therefore": the inhabitants were of small power because God had armed Sennacherib with His commission and strength, and had taken away their spirit and courage. The witnesses do not divide. Together they complete the argument begun in the previous verse: the Assyrian's victories prove nothing of his own greatness, for he triumphed only over peoples whom God had first turned to withered grass. The conqueror who boasted of felling cedars had in fact mown down grass already blighted, and the strength he gloried in was borrowed strength, lent for a purpose and revocable at will. What God gave He can withdraw; and the same hand that made the nations short-handed before Assyria can make Assyria short-handed in a night — a lesson the great king is about to learn in his own camp.
27:
The oracle presses closer, until the king's every movement lies open to God — "I know thy abode, and thy going out, and thy coming in, and thy rage against me" — and the witnesses dwell on this divine omniscience. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch read the verse as God's declaration that nothing of Sennacherib's is hidden: his sitting and rising, his marches and counter-marches, and above all his raging against the Lord, are all fully known, and because that rage has come up into God's ears the answering judgment of the next verse is already prepared.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott notes a textual wrinkle at the verse's opening. Literally it runs "and thy down-sitting, and thy going out, and thy coming in I know," and something seems to have fallen out at the start; probably, with Wellhausen, the words "and thine uprising is before me" were lost by a copyist because they resembled the ending of the previous verse, as the like language of Ps. 139:2 suggests. The thought, Ellicott says, is plain enough: I know all thy plans and doings, I see thy present rebellion against me; what thou hast hitherto done was done because I willed it, and now I will check thee.

The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley gives the verse its edge of irony: though thou dost not know me, yet I thoroughly know thee, and all thy designs and actions — thy secret contrivances in the place of thy abode, in thine own kingdom and court, and the execution of them abroad, what thou intendest in thy going out and with what further thoughts thou returnest home. The witnesses do not divide. Together they turn the tables on the great intelligence of the Assyrian empire: the king who prided himself on knowing the movements of every nation is himself watched, sitting and rising, by the God he affected not to know. Hezekiah had wished for word of the enemy's plans; but there was no need, Henry-like the point stands, for the eye of God was a constant spy upon him. The rage that ascended to heaven has been heard, and the One who knows the great king's every step will now turn those steps back the way they came.
28:
The sentence lands in an image at once brutal and comic — "I will put my hook in thy nose, and my bridle in thy lips" — and the witnesses draw out its force. The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke pictures the practice behind it: the words allude to the method of guiding a buffalo, which has a ring set in its nose and a cord or bridle attached, by which the beast is turned to right or left or round about at its driver's pleasure. The great king, for all his boasting, is to be led home like a hooked animal, his course no longer his own.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott grounds the figure first in the grammar — "because of thy rage and of thy self-confidence which is come up" into God's ears, comparing the arrogant ease of Isa. 32:9 — and then in fact rather than mere metaphor. The threat is no idle image: he compares the fate of Manasseh, led to Babylon with hooks in 2 Chron. 33:11. He records Keil's note that the metaphor is drawn from wild animals held thus in check — the ring in the nose of lions in Ezek. 19:4, the bridle in the mouth of intractable horses in Ps. 32:9 — all agreeing with the promise "I will turn thee back by the way thou camest."

The witnesses do not divide. Between Clarke's buffalo and Ellicott's lions and horses the picture is one: the conqueror who claimed to scale mountains and drink rivers dry is reduced to a beast on a lead, hauled around by the very God he blasphemed. The verse answers the whole swagger of the letter with a single humiliation. Sennacherib had lifted his voice against heaven; heaven will put a bit in his lips. He had come up by a certain road, breathing threats; he will go back by that same road, turned like a stubborn ox. And the manner of his going will make plain to every onlooker that the reins of the greatest empire on earth were never in the king's hands at all, but always in the hand of the Holy One of Israel.
29:
The word now turns from the king to Hezekiah, and gives him a sign in the rhythm of the harvest — "ye shall eat this year such as groweth of itself" — and the witnesses read it as a pledge lodged in the very fields. The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke explains the sign to whom it is addressed: it is to Hezekiah. Sennacherib had ravaged the country and the season for sowing was past, yet God would so bless the land that its self-sown growth would suffice; and though the second year was the sabbatical rest, when it was unlawful to plough or sow, even then by a special blessing the land would feed its people, until in the third year they should sow and plant and reap in abundance. This, Clarke says, was to be a sign that the deliverance came not by natural or casual means, for only miraculous care could feed an unploughed land and cut off a mighty army together.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott confirms that the prophet now addresses Hezekiah, and calls it "the sign" of the truth of the prophetic word — the foretelling of natural, nearer events that accredit the greater prediction. The two Hebrew terms he glosses as "after-growth" and "after-shoot," the growth that springs from old roots left in the ground, comparing Lev. 25:5 and Lev. 25:11; the prophecy was likely uttered in autumn, so that only one full year of husbandry would be lost.

The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley calls it an excellent sign, considering the waste the Assyrians had made and the people's being driven into their stronghold, unable to till or sow: yet this year they should have sufficient from what the earth yielded of itself, and the next, the year of release, again from its spontaneous growth, and in the third eat the fruit of their own labours — not sowing while another reaped, as of late, but enjoying their own. The witnesses do not divide. Together they show a promise written into the ground itself: the God who would slay the invader in a night would also feed the survivors through two barren years, so that the whole recovery, from the empty camp to the loaded threshing-floor, might be seen for what it was — not fortune, but the faithful care of God.
30:
The sign of the fields becomes a promise for the nation — "the remnant that is escaped of the house of Judah shall yet again take root downward, and bear fruit upward" — and the witnesses read the growing corn as a type of the growing people. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch explain that Isaiah uses the sign itself as a type: the remnant that has escaped the Assyrian will strike fresh roots and flourish once more, for Judah will not finally succumb to this judgment; those who have escaped destruction, the "escaped remnant," will go forth from Jerusalem, as the word is used in Isa. 4:2.

The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke reads the figure agriculturally: as the self-sown corn of the sign takes root in the soil and multiplies, so shall the Jewish people; their population, thinned by the sword, shall be greatly increased, and the desolations of the war soon forgotten. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott renders the phrase "shall add root," that is, take firmer root, like a tree that grips the ground more deeply after a storm — a figure, he notes, thoroughly in the style of Isaiah, comparing Isa. 6:13 and Isa. 27:6.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry lifts the image to its spiritual height. The prosperity here promised, he says, is described as taking root downward and bearing fruit upward — being well fixed and well provided for oneself, and then doing good to others; and such, he adds, is the prosperity of the soul, which takes root downward by faith in Christ and bears fruit upward in righteousness. John Wesley echoes the same balance: the remnant shall be well fixed and provided for themselves, and then do good to others. The witnesses do not divide. Together they read a single hope out of the storm: the people cut down to a stump will not perish but deepen, gripping the covenant ground more firmly for the very violence that struck them, and rising to bear the fruit of a restored nation. What looked like an end — a harried land, a fraction of a people — is in God's hand a fresh beginning, roots below and fruit above, the pledge of a recovery no Assyrian could prevent.
31:
The promise gathers to a certainty sealed by God's own zeal — "for out of Jerusalem shall go forth a remnant... the zeal of the LORD of hosts shall do this" — and the witnesses mark both the surviving few and the power behind them. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch take the verse as the ground of the preceding hope: from Jerusalem and Mount Zion a rescued remnant will go forth, and the guarantee is not Judah's strength but the jealousy of the Lord of hosts, which will surely accomplish it.

The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke reads the going-forth as increase: the Jews shall so multiply as to fill not only Jerusalem but the adjacent country; and he records, without endorsing, the view of some that "they that escape out of Mount Zion" foreshadows the apostles going forth to convert the Gentile world by the gospel. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott names the remnant as Isaiah's favourite doctrine, comparing Isa. 10:20-21: the ravaged land was to be newly stocked from Jerusalem, and "the zeal of the LORD of hosts" is another of the prophet's own phrases, the word "hosts," wanting in the common Hebrew text, being restored from many manuscripts and all the versions, as the parallel confirms in Isa. 37:32.

The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley draws out the comfort in the closing clause. That handful of Jews now shut up in Jerusalem shall go forth from their several habitations and, by God's singular blessing, increase exceedingly; and though, reflecting on their fewness and unworthiness, this might seem too great a blessing to expect, God will do it out of the zeal He has both for His own name and for the good of His undeserving people. The witnesses do not divide. Together they anchor the whole train of promise in a single word — zeal. The deliverance, the replanting, the multiplying of the remnant do not rest on any merit or might of Judah's, which is spent, but on the burning jealousy of the Lord of hosts for His own honour and His own people; and because it is His zeal, and not their strength, that undertakes the work, the outcome is as sure as God Himself.
32:
The oracle turns to plain particulars of the siege that will never happen — "he shall not come into this city, nor shoot an arrow there" — and the witnesses count the mounting denials. The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke lays out the fullest proofs that Jerusalem shall not be taken, telling them off one by one: Sennacherib shall not come into the city; he shall not be able to get near enough to shoot an arrow into it; he shall not bring an army before it; he shall not raise a mound or redoubt against it; nay, not even an Assyrian shield shall be seen in the country, not so much as a foraging party drawing near.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott reads the same clauses as a catalogue of the ordinary incidents of ancient warfare, each here forbidden: he shall not come to make his intended attack, nor shoot an arrow in open assault, nor come before it with shield as a storming party advancing to the walls under cover, nor cast up a bank against it in regular siege, comparing 2 Sam. 20:15 and Hab. 1:10 — incidents, he notes, that may be seen depicted on the Assyrian sculptures from Khorsabad. The witness of Keil and Delitzsch agrees, listing the same fourfold refusal as the substance of the divine word concerning the king of Assyria.

The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley adds the historical colour that makes the promise concrete: the army sent with Rabshakeh had not formed a close siege against the city but only disposed itself to block it at a distance, waiting, as they presumed, until the king should quickly take Libnah and Lachish. The witnesses do not divide. Together they let the sheer accumulation of negatives do its work: arrow, shield, bank, army, even a single shield in the land — every instrument of the besieger's craft is struck from Sennacherib's hand before it is lifted. The city that trembled at Rabshakeh's speech is told, in the most literal and military terms, that the dreaded siege will simply not take place; the great war-machine, so vividly carved on the palace walls of Nineveh, will not roll one arrow's flight nearer the walls of Zion.
33:
The promise is sealed with a reversal of the invader's whole march — "by the way that he came, by the same shall he return" — and the witnesses weigh the double sentence it carries. The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke catches the distinction folded into it: though his army shall not return, yet he himself shall return to Assyria; for, because of his blasphemy, Sennacherib is not to fall here with his host but is reserved for a more ignominious death at home. The road that brought him up in pride will carry him back in shame, and the grave that waits for him is not before Jerusalem but at Nineveh.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott confirms the reading and corrects the text. The versions and the parallel in Isaiah rightly have "he came," where the Hebrew here reads "he cometh"; and he links the clause to the earlier threat, "I will turn thee back by the way by which thou camest," in v.28. "And unto this city he shall not come," Ellicott adds, repeats the assurance of v.32 — the denial pressed twice for the comfort of a frightened people. The witness of Keil and Delitzsch reads the verse the same way, as the Lord's own word that the king shall retire by his own road and not enter the city.

The witnesses do not divide, and the verse's brevity carries a large meaning. Sennacherib had come up boasting that he had scaled mountains and drunk rivers dry; he is to go back the way he came, having accomplished none of it, turned about like the hooked beast of the earlier image. Yet the sentence is not simple mercy: his life is spared here only that judgment may find him elsewhere, "reserved," in Clarke's grim phrase, "for a more ignominious death." The retreat by the same road is thus both a rescue for Jerusalem and a summons for the king — the city delivered, the blasphemer sent home to the sword of his own sons. The God who turns the great army back also appoints the place and manner of the great king's fall, and neither the deliverance nor the doom will miss its mark.
34:
The oracle closes with God's pledge to stand as the city's own shield — "I will defend this city, to save it, for mine own sake, and for my servant David's sake" — and the witnesses dwell on the two reasons named. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott renders the verb more vividly: "I will cover this city with a shield," comparing the same tender image in Isa. 31:5, where the Lord defends Jerusalem as birds flying over their nest, and in 2 Kings 20:6. The ground "for my servant David's sake" he refers to the ancient promise, citing 1 Kings 11:12-13 and the covenant of 2 Samuel 7.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry draws out the security of the pledge. God Himself undertakes to defend the city, and that person, that place, cannot but be safe whose protection He undertakes. Henry hears in the two clauses the two great springs of all deliverance. "I will do it for my own sake," God says, to make Himself an everlasting name — for His reasons of mercy are fetched from within Himself; and "for my servant David's sake," not for any merit of David but for the promise made to him and the covenant of the sure mercies of David. And Henry lifts the second reason to its fulfilment: all the deliverances of the church, he says, are wrought for the sake of Christ, the Son of David, in whom the covenant with David finally stands.

The witness of Keil and Delitzsch reads the verse in the same key, as the Lord's own undertaking to save the city for His own sake and His servant David's. The witnesses do not divide. Together they show that the safety of Jerusalem rests on no strength of its walls nor wisdom of its king, but on two immovable things — the honour of God's name and the faithfulness of God's covenant. Zion is spared because God will not let His name be trampled by a blasphemer, and because He has bound Himself to David by a promise that reaches beyond David to David's greater Son. On such a ground the city is impregnable; the shield over Jerusalem is the covenant-keeping God Himself, and the next verse will show how swiftly He can make that shield felt.
35:
The word is fulfilled in a single dreadful night — "the angel of the LORD went out, and smote in the camp of the Assyrians a hundred fourscore and five thousand" — and the witnesses stand amazed, dividing chiefly over the manner of the stroke. The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry marvels at the swiftness: the word was no sooner spoken than the work was done. Hezekiah had no force to sally out; God did it not by sword or bow but sent His angel, a destroying angel, in the dead of night, such an angel as slew the firstborn of Egypt, so that it was not by the sword of a mighty man or a mean man, as in Isa. 31:8. Josephus, Henry notes, thought it a pestilential disease; and when the besieged arose early, behold, they were all dead corpses, the stout-hearted spoiled and sunk into their last sleep, as in Ps. 76:5.

The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke leans openly to the natural means: he believes the angel or messenger of the Lord was simply a suffocating, pestilential wind that destroyed the army as in a moment, without noise or warning — the very "blast" threatened in v.7 — Rabshakeh perishing with the rest, and only Sennacherib and a few witnesses spared. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott gathers the evidence: the destroying angel is he who smote the firstborn in Exod. 12:23 and Israel after David's census in 2 Sam. 24:15-17, passages that favour the view of a pestilence, though others have suggested a simoom or storm; and he holds that a supernatural causation is involved in any case, both in the number slain in one night and in the exact coincidence with Isaiah's word. Herodotus, he adds, fixed the scene in Egypt, and the camp, as vv.32-33 show, was not before Jerusalem. John Wesley says only that it was such an angel as destroyed the firstborn of Egypt, and that those who arose were the few left alive.

The witnesses divide over the instrument but not the Author. Whether the stroke fell by a personal angel, as Henry and Wesley press, or by a pestilential wind, as Clarke and Ellicott incline, all agree it was God's direct act, foretold to the letter and executed in a night. See how weak, Henry cries, is the mightiest of men before almighty God; the pride and blasphemy of the king are punished in the destruction of his army, and all those lives are sacrificed to God's glory and Zion's safety. The camp that had gathered to swallow Jerusalem becomes a field of corpses, and the boast of the letter is answered not by argument but by an empty morning.
36:
The great king's retreat is told in a rush of verbs — "so Sennacherib king of Assyria departed, and went, and returned, and dwelt at Nineveh" — and the witnesses hear in the very piling of words the haste and confusion of his flight. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch observe that this divine judgment compelled him to withdraw without delay; the heaping up of "he decamped, departed, and returned" expresses the hurry of the march home, and "he remained in Nineveh" implies both that he lived some while longer and that he never again undertook an expedition against Judah, Nineveh being the city of Gen. 10:11.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott parses the movement: "departed and went" means he broke up his camp and marched, with a stop to be made at "returned"; and "dwelt at Nineveh" implies that he did not again invade the west. Ellicott adds the historian's caution that Sennacherib records five later expeditions to the east, north, and south of his dominions, but these were nothing to the peoples of Palestine — his arm against Judah was broken. Nineveh he locates by the great mounds on the east bank of the Tigris, opposite Mosul, the Ninua of the inscriptions. The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke notes simply that Nineveh was the capital of the Assyrian empire.

The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley catches the psychology the crowded verbs betray: the manner of the expression intimates the great disorder and distraction of mind the king was in. The witnesses do not divide. Together they read the broken, hurrying clauses as a portrait of a shattered pride: the monarch who had come up boasting that no mountain or river could stop him now decamps, marches, returns, and shuts himself in his capital, never to trouble Judah again. The rivers he claimed to drink dry did not stop him; the God he blasphemed did, in a single night. And the sequel, that he "dwelt at Nineveh" and there stayed, is itself a quiet monument to the oracle: the way that he came, by that same way he returned, and the door that closed behind him at Nineveh was the door of judgment, waiting only for the last verse to open.
37:
The chapter ends where blasphemy always ends — in the ruin of the blasphemer — as Sennacherib falls murdered at his idol's feet, and the witnesses read both the history and the justice of it. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch tell it plainly: as he was worshipping in the house of Nisroch his god, his sons Adrammelech and Sharezer smote him with the sword and fled into the land of Ararat, and Esarhaddon his son reigned in his stead. Nisroch, they judge, was an eagle-deity, the vulture-headed winged figure so often carved on the Assyrian monuments and there shown victorious over other mythical beasts, a type of the supreme god.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott dates the murder some twenty years after the disaster and works at the corrupt name and the fugitives. "Nisroch" he thinks corrupt — the versions give varying forms, and one late text names Dagon — while Wellhausen would read a form involving Asshur; Adrammelech he ties to 2 Kings 17:31, and Sharezer he takes as part of the fuller Assyrian name Nergal-sar-usur, "Nergal protect the king." The sons "escaped into the land of Armenia," that is Ararat, the Assyrian Urartu; and Esarhaddon, the Assur-aha-iddina of the inscriptions, reigned from 681 to 668 B.C.

The wesleyan-arminian and puritan-evangelical witnesses read the providence in the crime. Adam Clarke reports the rabbinic tale that the sons had learned their father meant to sacrifice them to his god and struck first — a knot the rabbins cut rather than untie. Matthew Henry weighs the justice: the sons were monstrous to kill their own father, and in the act of his devotion, yet God was righteous in it, for justly are sons suffered to rebel against the father who begot them when he was in rebellion against the God that made him; and he notes that Esarhaddon his successor first sent Assyrian colonies into Samaria, as 2 Kings 17:24 and Ezra 4:2 attest. John Wesley draws the moral home: the God of Israel had done enough to convince him He was the only true God, yet he persisted in his idolatry, so that justly is his blood mingled with his sacrifices, who would not be convinced by so dear-bought a demonstration of the folly of worshipping idols. The witnesses do not divide. Together they close the chapter on a single irony: the king who mocked the living God and trusted his eagle-idol is cut down while bowing to it, by the hands of his own sons, and the blasphemy that opened the chapter is answered, at its end, in his blood.
